【Day 29】Ten Years as an Engineer and the Future
This series has been rewritten and published as a book, currently available at

(in Traditional Chinese). (Updated 2026/05/07)
The book version was essentially rewritten from scratch — I filled in the parts of the story I couldn't fully tell during the original IT Home serialization, and cleaned up sections where the writing wasn't precise enough. It's a version I'm much happier with.
If you enjoy this post and want more, or want the polished version, the book is the way to go. The blog and IT Home will keep the original serialized version for archive purposes — please treat the book as the authoritative reference.
As of today, I've been a software engineer for ten years.
The main goal of joining this Ironman challenge was to debrief these ten years of knowledge and experience.

From a clueless beginner who only had love for games, all the way to today —
This road was full of setbacks, but also full of rewards.
"Did you ever think about quitting engineering?"
Honestly, plenty of times. But whenever I wanted to quit, I'd remember my original motivations —
The first time I felt the excitement of playing games
The first time I saw the screen interact with me — surprise.
My original motivation never dying is the reason I made it here.
From PHP engineer to frontend engineer — could this path possibly be easy?

My programming foundation made learning slightly faster, but the challenges were still many:
JavaScript's quirks, frontend ecosystem's rapid changes, various framework learning curves... Easy? Not at all XD.
So what did I do to become a frontend engineer? Quick debrief:
Watched various frontend courses (YouTube, Udemy, Hahow, HiSKIO, etc.)
, and other social media to share knowledge and experience
Attended various frontend events (tech conferences, community meetups, etc.)
Hosted small book clubs and sharing sessions
Eventually became a Mentor — improving my own tech and knowledge through sharing and teaching

Did I become amazing because of all this?
Or a famous figure in the frontend space?

I don't think so.
Learning is endless — these ten years I've learned a lot, but there's still so much I haven't touched, countless areas worth exploring.
Looking back at this ten-year journey — is that the wrap-up?
Of course not. It's just a new beginning. Especially with AI's rapid development, our lives and work have changed dramatically.
I think the future will be like this:
Knowledge access becomes easier and cheaper
Anyone can build their own product
AI will heavily replace repetitive work
People will focus more on creative work
Everyone must learn to use AI tools to assist their work
The future isn't humans replaced by AI — it's humans who can use AI.
No matter how advanced AI gets, fundamentals still matter, because AI still hallucinates.
We need enough knowledge to judge whether the output is correct.
Without judgment, AI's output becomes a black box — even like black magic. You can't verify correctness, and end up making bigger mistakes in work.

I keep conveying this to students:
Rather than just teaching them how to use AI tools, I'd rather they learn to judge whether AI output is correct, and
face AI tools with the right mindset
You read that right. After AI emerged, many students lost learning motivation — many stay anxious for long periods, worried they'll be replaced by AI.
Rather than worry, understand how AI works and try using it for things. You'll discover — AI isn't omnipotent. It has pros and cons, and still needs human intelligence to assist and guide.

For replacement, I think it'll take time.
(Though I also wish AI would quickly replace my daily work, so I could spend more time on what I love XD.)
During this period I've built tools with AI:
These are things I'd long wanted to build, but lacked time to. AI has let me finish them faster, freeing time for other things.
Of course, this series didn't cover every topic, e.g.:
Servers (Linux, Nginx, Apache, Docker, Kubernetes, etc.)
Software dev process (Agile, Scrum, Kanban, etc.)
Software design patterns (MVC, MVVM, Singleton, Factory, etc.)
CI/CD (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD, etc.)
Other languages (Python, Go, Rust, Java, C#, etc.)
I learned all of these over the ten years.
Covering each would have needed way more than Day 30 to wrap up. To protect my liver, I picked a few interesting fundamentals to share, hoping to build initial understanding.
Other deeper knowledge — I'll leave it to you to explore!
Will I join the next Ironman? Honestly I don't know. I just hope, having read this series, you got something out of it and grew a bit.
After all, it took me a long time to slowly get to where I am today.

As long as you persist,
I believe you can also become who you want to be — or the software engineer of your dreams. :)
I'm Ray. See you next time, maybe.
